content:
Good afternoon folks. I'm going to check Twitter quickly to ensure you can hear me and see five charts on
my screen. If everything is fine, a '5 by five' confirmation would be appreciated.
- Starting with the Dollar Index
- Followed by Euro Dollar
- Then Pound Dollar
- S&P 500 futures
- NASDAQ
- Concluding with Gold

- WEEKLY CHART ANALYSIS AND CLEAN CHART PHILOSOPHY
We're starting with the Dollar Index on the weekly chart. This area up here, I've mentioned this before.
We're working with a virgin chart; everything is fresh. If I were by myself looking at things, I wouldn't be
annotating the chart this way. Instead, I'd be writing down the levels on my notepad.
- This candle's low
- This candle's high
- The range between those two respective price points, which is the consequent encroachment here, at
108.85.

I don't keep them on my chart because I want to be flexible. I've learned that if I have the levels on my
chart, I can tend to marry the idea, especially if it's something I really want to do or if I've talked about it
openly and publicly shared it. Seeing it on my chart keeps it in my subconscious, making me want to see it
traded to, which can cause me to:
- Ignore real-time changes in sentiment
- Ignore changes in order flow
- Ignore shifts in the narrative
Having a clean chart makes it easier for me to switch up, change gears, and reverse my entire analysis on
the spot. Initially, you shouldn't try to do this. Without experience, you might change your mind too soon, too
many times, leading to account drawdown or blowouts. Keeping charts clean helps me remain fluid and not
marry to a specific level.

- INEFFICIENCY AND PRICE ACTION REVIEW
That imbalance, I have that noted. Looking back through price action, we had this small inefficiency here.
We discussed how it could potentially trade back down into that if we were going to correct. It did that and
went past it slightly, which is permissible. There isn't much inefficiency to speak of beyond that in this area.
There's a small one, but price didn't quite get into it between this candle's high and that candle's low. We
didn't even get below this candle's low for it to be a factor. It just overshot this gap and then went higher.

- CONSEQUENT ENCROACHMENT AND DAILY CLOSE CONFIRMATION
One of the things I'm interested in is how we went right back up to the previous week's consequent
encroachment. The Dollar Index went right back up to that level, stopped after going slightly past it, and
then came off the high of the week. If we start pressing into this, it would need to close on a daily chart.
This is a weekly chart, as seen in the upper left-hand corner. If we look at this in terms of the upper
quadrant, it would be here at 107.05. If we have a daily candle close above that, I would expect it to trade
above this high and start working into this inefficiency.

- RANGING OUTLOOK UNTIL DAILY CONFIRMATION
As long as we remain below this upper quadrant, specifically 107.05, I'm anticipating it ranging between the
low of last week and that upper quadrant level. In other words, we're looking at this as a range until we get
a closing basis above this particular level here. That close has to be on a daily chart, not a weekly. When
the daily chart closes above this price level, my focus will be expecting it to go above and trade up into this
area here. If it just goes to that level and keeps going, taking out that high, then it still remains the same: I
would expect it up here.

- GEOPOLITICAL FACTORS AND CAUTION
For the sake of leaving this pre-week open it's 12:52 p.m. on Sunday, my local time in New York, before
the opening bell a couple of hours from now I don't know where we're going to open. There are a lot of
things going on around the world between now and then. Some expected events could unfold, like what we
saw two Fridays ago with the Middle East and Gaza. There's a lot of tension and potential spillover into
other nations. This concern is ramping up the flight to quality or safe haven aspect of the Dollar Index or the
dollar.

- MARKET BEHAVIOR AND YEAR-END ACTIVITY
The reason why I'm calling the market ranging between these two levels here this week's high that we
just closed and the low of that week is because we're going to work inside that range until we get a
definitive close on a daily basis above this upper quadrant. Ultimately, this is where I believe we're drawing
to; I have not called a top in the Dollar Index and I'm not trying to do that. We saw it trade up into that level
two weeks ago, hitting it, then retracing, and coming back to consequent encroachment here.

- RATIONALE AMIDST GEOPOLITICAL VOLATILITY
What causes me to call for a trading range environment until there's a definitive close above the upper
quadrant? You're probably asking, 'What makes you think that, ICT? What makes you think it would do that,
not just keep going higher?' It can go higher; I prefer that it would. But because I don't know where we're
going to open and I don't know what's likely to occur due to some kind of terrorist action or war response,
all those things will rev up these sentiment ideas, and fear and greed will start coming into the marketplace,
as we'll discuss with gold later. That makes it a little different in terms of normal market delivery.

- CONSOLIDATION TRIGGERS AND HIGHER DOLLAR BIAS
There's a lot of fear in the marketplace right now, and we're going into the last quarter of the year where
markets have a lot more activity. Traders will try to make money before the close of the year because, as
we get closer to December, new risk is less likely to come into the marketplace. It's a winding down as we
get closer to the second week of December, and then we start expecting markets to soften up, barring any
crazy 9/11-esque type event that would cause markets to go haywire. Why the range here? Because we
didn't trade above this weekly high; we went just to the consequent encroachment, slightly above it, but
failed to get above this high here and ultimately stopped with no close above the consequent
encroachment or midpoint of this longest wick. If this would have traded and closed above this one here, I
would say we're going to roll right into follow-through from Jump Street Sunday later today and start
working towards here, gravitating up into this area all week long. But because we did not get above two
weeks ago's high and because we didn't close up above the consequent encroachment of the week prior to
the one we just closed, this tends to create a need for consolidation.

- LONG-TERM BIAS AND CONFIRMATION CRITERIA
If I'm wrong, it's no skin off my back because either way, I'm subscribing to the idea that the dollar goes
higher. There's no plan A, plan B. If it goes down, I'm right. I'm saying I want it to go higher because of the
delivery of it not getting above this two weeks ago's high and the week ago prior to the one we just closed,
it didn't close above the midpoint. It should have closed above 106.76. It should have, but it didn't; it just
returned right back to consequent encroachment and came off the highs. But in this run higher, it didn't
even get above the high of two weeks prior, which is here at 106.839. The consequent encroachment of
this wick on this weekly candlestick comes in at 106.761, which is lower than the previous week. But the
week we just closed wasn't able to close above consequent encroachment, and it wasn't able to trade
above two weeks prior.

- PRE-MARKET ANTICIPATION AND ENGAGEMENT FILTERS
So it set up a scenario where I could be wrong; it could trade down and go lower and work inside this area
here. I'm not saying that's going to happen. If it does that, I'm wrong that's very finite, very specific. If it
trades down to there, then I'm wrong about my pre-week opening analysis. What I'm expecting and
anticipating going into the market before we even see the opening price later today on Sunday is that we're
going to remain in this range until something drives us above the upper quadrant of this wick right in here,
and it has to close on the daily chart. Once it does that, then I'm confident that we're likely to continue
higher, and then we'll work towards those higher timeframe imbalances or imbalance rather on the weekly
chart of NASDAQ, which is this one right up here. That's a long way around the barn to explain why I
believe what I'm expecting and why I'm comfortable working with this idea.

- SELL SIDE LIQUIDITY AND BREAKAWAY GAP
Let's drop down to the daily chart. Here we have the daily in that range, and it's nice that we came down,
took out that sell side here, had a really strong reaction here on Thursday and Friday. It just looks too much
like it wants to consolidate a little bit more. We have an imbalance right there, so we could drop that into
this. I prefer it stays open, to be honest with you. What I'm highlighting here, I'd like to see this stay
absolutely untouched, not covered up at all, not repriced, just simply leave it open as a breakaway gap.
That's what I would prefer to see. This buy side-sell side inefficiency, I want to see it act as a breakaway
gap, meaning that I don't want to see the entirety of that range closed in.

- CAUTION AND TRADING FILTERS
Can it come back down and touch just below this candle's low, below the 106.78 level, or below the 106.29,
which is the low of Friday, and then go higher? Yes. I'd prefer that it didn't, but because I don't know where
we're opening on Sunday, and I'm not going to trade on Sunday, and I'm not going to trade on Monday
without having something that's so obvious. So right now, I'm willing to just lay down and not take any
action on Monday. I want to see what we do with this new sentiment over the weekend. There was
supposed to be some big global response, and it was rather mute, thankfully not a whole lot of things going
on. Doesn't mean that it won't happen at a later time, as I believe it will, but it just means that it may have
lulled people into thinking that we're okay, there's no reason to be concerned.

- MARKET SENTIMENT AND EVENT RISKS
People like myself, who have been around for a little bit longer than the average person trading today, we
know better. We want to see the market open up and see what they do with this information, this insight,
this new sentiment. Does fear grip the marketplace when they send it higher in the Dollar Index, signifying
that it's a flight to quality, a safe haven move? I don't know, because we see gold rally, and this is why we'll
talk about gold in terms of an event-driven market. But right now, the Dollar Index could just hammer
around inside this range here. It could very well come down and overlap this fair value gap.

- SPECIFIC ENTRY CONDITIONS AND RISK MANAGEMENT
And guess what that means? It means I'm not taking that as a long idea on dollar and short on Euro or
cable. Why? Because I want to see it do what I mentioned on the weekly chart. This is where everybody
likes to take things out of context and say, 'But you said this and you're wrong!' No, you're not listening. I'm
not interested in taking a trade this week until we have a daily close above the upper quadrant on that
weekly range that we just showed on the weekly long-wicked candle two weeks ago. It needs to close on a
daily chart above that level. When it does that, then I'm interested in trading. If it goes through it intraday
and keeps on going higher than that previous week's high two weeks ago, then okay, that's the same thing
for me. But the bare minimum is that I want to see that close above the upper quadrant of the range of that
weekly wick two weeks ago on the weekly chart. So I don't even care if it drops down into this; it doesn't
signify a buy for me. It just means, 'Okay, once it gets there, I want to see what it does. I want more
information.'

- CAUTIOUS APPROACH AMIDST GLOBAL INSTABILITY
Why am I doing that? Because I'm being cautious. Because I'm going to be risking real money, not demo.
I'm not going to be trading a market replay; I'm going to be doing something with real money. Therefore, I
want to have a little bit more assurance behind all the things that are going on around the world. All it takes,
folks, all it takes is for something to blow up at the right place, at the right time, in the right country, and all
hell breaks loose, and these markets will start doing things that you've never seen before. So you have to
be very cautious. There are high levels of risk right now that you have to appreciate, and you just can't
think, 'Well, all these things going on causes great market conditions.' Well, yeah, after the event takes
place, yeah. But you don't know what's going to pop off next, so you have to be very careful, very, very
careful.

- FOREX VS. FUTURES & MARKET SURVIVABILITY
Hopefully, I made that very clear. Just because it comes back down here, I don't see that as a buy for dollar
and sell for euro dollar. If it does that, I'm willing to let it go without me, not that I'm even trading Forex or
Pound Dollar or Euro Dollar. But for those that are following me, it's predominantly Forex folks that follow
me now. But I'm trying to build the audience up with the more gentlemanly traded market or asset class,
which is Futures. Even if Forex falls away, you will always have Index Futures to trade and commodities, so
that's a market that won't ever go away.

- IMBALANCE AND PRICE ACTION EXPECTATIONS
The imbalance trading into it, it's just something for me to watch and see: does it respect it? If it trades
below it on a daily chart and closes below this buy side imbalance efficiency, then that means I'm probably
going to see a lower run here, and it would need to go through just a very, very small little movement and
then snap back higher and still resuming with the idea that if it has a daily close above the upper quadrant
of last week's range, or two weeks ago rather. Let me go back, I probably just confused you. It needs to get
above the upper quadrant of that wick right here, which is again why I write my levels down, and it needs to
close above this price level here before me to engage anything. So I've placed a filter on my engagement
this week, and unless it does that, I'm not touching it.

- ACCEPTANCE OF MISSED TRADES AND EXTREME CAUTION
What happens if it sells off and goes a lot lower? I've missed it, and I've done nothing with it, and I'm okay
with that. How's that for logic? I am very, very careful right now because I don't trust that something wild
and unexpected going off somewhere won't occur. I believe it will. So because of that, I'm being very
cautious about what I'm anticipating. And if it does this very thing here, I will feel confident that it's going to
go higher on the dollar, and it might be a short period of time where gold and dollar move together, which is
not historically what you would expect. One goes higher, one goes lower; they're usually diametrically
opposed. But because we watch Thursday and Friday, Dollar Index...

- DOLLAR-GOLD TANDEM MOVEMENT
Let me just do gold right after dollar because it's a little bit easier for me to segue that way. I won't forget
about it. Because the chances are I might do it, I might get into a rant a little bit after we close up with
NASDAQ, and I'll forget. And then I'll be all upset about it, and I won't be going and doing another
additional review. I'll just be mad about it, and I'll be tweeting to you about what it is, and I hate my evening
because I didn't do what I wanted to do. So we saw Thursday and Friday trade higher with the Dollar Index.
So if we look at gold right out of this market into what would normally be a diametrically opposed delivery,
look what we're seeing here: gold and dollar are moving in tandem. That's something that I don't like to see.
I'd like to see where there is a diametrically opposed delivery, where a symmetrical market would be where
dollar goes lower, gold is permitted to go higher. It doesn't mean that it will go higher; it just means that it's
the most likely won't go down. It can consolidate and slowly work its way higher. But either way, it should
not be moving in the same direction.

- EVENT-DRIVEN MARKET AND GLOBAL ECONOMIC SHIFTS
Now, what causes these types of things? It's an event-driven market. Gold is highly manipulated, and it's
also treated sometimes as a safe haven market as well. Couple that with the fact that there are central
banks around the world that are really trying to build up their gold supplies because they're going to be
doing central bank digital currencies, and they're going to try, like the BRICS nations, they're trying to sell
the idea that their currency that they're going to release later on will be a gold-backed currency. So it would
demolish the dollar because it's really built and based on an idea that's an illusion. This is what we say it's
worth; do you believe us? That's the donor system. It's not like it used to be when it took us off the gold
standard; it became an illusion. And then we created this relationship with Saudi Arabia. We said, 'Look,
we're going to protect you as long as you make everyone buy your oil with dollars.'

- ECONOMIC DIRE STRAITS AND CURRENCY DECOUPLING
Which when you think about it, when you really can come to terms with it, it's really asinine. Like, that's how
is that like? That's that shouldn't have been like that ever. And now that country is departing from that
arrangement, not entirely, but it's allowing, and I think what it's doing, it's got its toe in the water to see what
will we do in response? Well, when we're staring at Russia and China together in a fortified union building
an opposition to the greenback or the dollar, we're doing nothing. That's what we're doing. We're sitting
back and letting them do whatever they want to do because we're technically outnumbered, and it's been
checkmate for two years now, and nobody really understands that. And all the other nations are slowly
siding with those BRICS nations, and you're actually seeing the combination of that last beast that the
Book of Daniel talks about, and I'm not going to get into that here, but that's what I see happening, and we
are in dire straits economically. When that becomes full on, and they release their currency where
everybody sees it, it's gold-backed, there's nothing holding up our dollar then, and then we will see
Venezuela 2.0 right here in the United States, and it's going to happen real fast like that.

So because of all those factors that are weighing heavily on my decision-making, that's why I'm not also
trading in Forex because I think there's going to be a decoupling that's going to happen so sudden that
brokerage firms, financial institutions, banks, traders they're all going to get destroyed real fast because
it's going to be just like the Euro Dollar and the Swiss Franc when there was the decoupling that
completely... it was like a nuclear bomb going off. I mean, business as usual after that happened, but it was
very scary seeing huge runs, thousand-pip swings instantaneously. You're not ready for that, and I don't
care if you're trading with a funded account and you think you're safe, those things won't be around after
that. That's what I'm trying to explain to you. Like, I'm not willing to invest in Forex because of that very
thing that's looming. That's not to say that that can't create reactions and ripples so large that they wouldn't
be painful in Futures or commodities, or... well, I said it, Index Futures, but in the bond market, they'll have
them too. But currencies are the... that's where the target is right now. The target is currencies. They want
to have a smaller basket to choose from in terms of currencies, and the first thing they want to take away is
the dollar and the influence that we've had over everyone else's nation and tried to be the world police and
use it like a sharp stick and say, 'You better do this or else.'

section_title: GLOBAL POWER SHIFTS AND PERSONAL PREPAREDNESS
content:
And now all these other nations that are just as powerful, if not now more powerful than us, because
they've outnumbered us on the chessboard, there's too many superpowers that are standing against us in
opposition saying, 'We don't need your dollar.' And the average person isn't aware of that; they don't think
that way because they have their dollars coming to them in their paycheck, even though it's more expensive
to buy everything, that's the extent of their visibility. They don't see anything past that. So there's a lot of
concern that all of you should have, and you should be trying to do whatever you can to fortify your home
with things that you may need because our currency could be devalued rather quickly, and what you think
you have in a bank versus what you actually have to spend with it is totally two different things.

- IMMEDIATE OUTLOOK
And that's further communicated given the total global climate where we are. There's a lot of saber rattling
and war talk amongst nations, and gold rallying up like that while the dollar is going higher too. So I want to
see what continues on Monday, Sunday night, a little bit later today when we see the market open up. Does
gold go higher? Does dollar go higher? Because one of the two is going to not do it; one's going to give up.
And I'm thinking that at least for the first couple days, gold will continue higher, and dollar can consolidate.
Now, do you see why all I said about the Dollar Index? I just can't simply say it in a five-second expos of
what I'm contemplating. There's a lot of factors that weigh on why I said the very things I said in just a few
minutes of talking about, 'I want to see it stay in a range,' or 'I'm anticipating it stay in range.'

section_title: GOLD AS A CATALYST FOR DOLLAR MOVEMENT
content:
But my focus is what we're seeing in gold because gold's going to be the catalyst behind the follow-through
higher on dollar, or if it's continuing higher for gold and gold wants to run for the buy side here, if it wants to
run there, that means that it might cause dollar to stay in that range I outlined. And then once it gets here,
does it power higher? And if it does, then it's going to probably send that Dollar Index down into that daily
fair value gap, which again still would not mean a buy for me. I would need to weigh out what gold is doing
at the same time that the Dollar Index is dropping down into that daily fair value gap. So there's this teeter
- totter effect. I'm balancing out two markets that are closely correlated, but they're usually inversely
correlated, meaning one goes up, the other goes down. But because of all the things that are going on right
now, they're moving in tandem. That's not a normal function or symmetrical market.

You want to see a market environment where you're willing to take on risk where they're doing the opposite
of one another. That is a symmetrical market. That means everything is on the level, meaning that if you're
bullish on dollar, that should be weakness on gold, or there should be no strength in gold going higher, and
all foreign currencies should move lower. Equities should start going lower and have a short-lived rally; if it
does rally, they're suspect rallies, they're fake rallies, they're just going up to go into an inefficiency and
quickly reject it or go up above a short-term high then reject it. That's how you use the Dollar Index. But just
because we're both bullish or bearish on the Dollar Index, if you're not factoring in the effects of gold,
because gold is the qualifier of dollar, you're not just simply looking at Dollar Index and thinking, 'Oh well,
it's going higher.' You can't just look at Dollar Index going up and assume everything else mark-to-market
should go lower. It doesn't, it doesn't work like that.

Forex pairs and Forex currencies, those types of markets, yes, they many times will work mark-to-market.
You mark-to-market, it'll be usually on par Euro for certain because the dollar is heavily weighted with the
Euro. But other currencies like the pound tend to do whatever it wants to do. Pound is like the NASDAQ of
Forex. It might bend the knee to lower prices on whole, but it's going to buck back when it wants to and be
unruly. And also when it does move, it's going to move at an accelerated pace, just like cable does. Cable is
Pound Dollar. So when the market's predisposed to go higher or lower relative to the Dollar Index going
higher or lower, Euro is tending to move mark-to-market with what the Dollar Index does. But if it's bearish
for Euro, bullish for dollar, Cable can over-deliver and be exaggerated to the downside, which is when I was
trading Forex, that's why I elected more times to trade the Pound Dollar than the Euro. Euro tended to be a
little bit too slow for my liking, which is why I trade NASDAQ more so than I trade the ES, even though they
should move in tandem with my overall macro analysis. I tend to use the NASDAQ because if I'm on side
and I'm correct, the moves can be exaggerated in my favor.

section_title: NASDAQ VS. ES AND MARKET CHARACTERISTICS
content:
Now, it's a double-edged sword because if I'm wrong in my analysis, if I'm wrong in my entry, then that
means I'm going to sit through a little bit more drawdown or have a larger loss than that would have been
taken if I would have traded simply the ES because it's going to be a slower market. But each one of them
has their inherent risks and their own characteristics, and I like to be in a market that's going to be the
exaggeration, the one that does more than you would expect. That's always a good thing. Anyway, that's
enough for gold. That's what I'm using it for, that's how I'm interpreting it, that's how I've used gold in the
analysis of dollar before we got to this market here. So now there's no necessity for me to go back to dollar;
I've closed that chapter and I've talked about gold, so now we can go into Euro.

- WEEKLY CHART INTRODUCTION
All right, so here is the weekly chart of Euro Dollar or Fiber. Let's zoom in a little bit. I hope that made
sense. I mean, I know some of you are really, really new, and it might have went over your head or been a
little bit confusing, but I promise you, it's not all that complicated. But knowing or having the experience to
anticipate these types of things, it's not reasonable to expect a new trader to think that way because no one
is actively teaching that part of it. I mean, everybody knows that dollar up is risk off and everything else
should go down, but it's not simply limited to that. There's a whole lot of other things that have to come
along with that.

- UNDISCUSSED MARKET FACTORS
And in my experience, with a lot of books, reading stuff that really is regurgitation, I didn't find a lot of value
in that stuff. So there's a whole lot of other factors that aren't ever really discussed that I just shared with
you, so hopefully, it'll carry you through your career with help. So we had this volume imbalance; we traded
up into that. It's just all old news, and it's moved lower. We went below here, these relative equal lows; we
had sell side taken. I did not say that we were at the bottom. And the same thing I'm saying in terms of the
Dollar Index, I think that we gradually move higher on dollar.

- GEOPOLITICAL IMPACT AND PRICE ACCELERATION
But as to what catalyst that is, what's the driver? Because if we start seeing a lot of terrorist attacks, if we
start seeing a lot of those things happening in mainland US, the Dollar Index is going to go parabolic; it's
going to go straight up, and that also might accompany a rise parabolic in gold. If we see those types of
things occurring, like buses being blown up, buildings being blown up, things happening in the New York
Stock Exchange or near the New York Stock Exchange, or army bases, military bases, and things of that
nature, or large gatherings of people. If we start seeing the stuff that goes on in Gaza and in Israel, if that
starts happening here, that's going to cause so much chaos and fear, and these markets will rev that up,
and it'll exaggerate it and cause people to be fearful about their money, and that's what that's what it'll be
used for.

- ULTIMATE DRAW AND MARKET MAKERS
So if we get all those things happening, Euro could accelerate rather quickly right down into this area here.
Ultimately, before the end of the year, I think we're going to trade into this. That was my quarterly shift
analysis; I was expecting this type of thing to occur. I'm still selling the idea that that's what I believe is likely
to occur. I believe that the Dollar Index will go up into the inefficiency I mentioned. So between both of
those two markets, we're seeing where... I shouldn't have it this color because it doesn't stay with the
historical use of the colors. So I think that if we have a ramp up of violence and things that are hopefully not
going to happen, but if it does, that's going to cause the markets to go through a state of panic, and panic
will be accelerated by the fast delivery in the markets, and they will use that going into next year. So how
they how they being the market makers, the central banks, that's who the market makers are in case you
want to know who they are it's them. So when that happens, if it does happen, we'll be looking for Euro to
try to trade down into this area here. Doesn't mean it won't go here and stop; it just means that I see it
wanting to get down here, okay, in this area. And because of all the things that are going on in the world, it
could be delivered rather quickly.

- INVERSE EXPECTATIONS WITH DOLLAR
So everything I just mentioned with the dollar early on, just apply that to this in terms of expectations, but
just reverse it in terms of the direction because I'm expecting higher dollar. I'm not expecting lower dollar.
I'm not calling a top in the Dollar Index. With all the things going on around the world, there's absolutely
zero reason to anticipate or expect a high in the dollar to be formed, period.

- INEFFICIENCY AND MARKET STRUCTURE
All right, so here's the daily chart on Euro. And there's no, there's no inefficiency in here that I feel is
noteworthy to anticipate a retracement down then go higher. Normally, let's say it this way: we could see
this low, high, lower low. If we're in a bullish market, if we're in a bullish condition where dollar is absolutely
bearish, then this run up here above this high, this would have been viewed by me as a shift in market
structure. For the folks that ask this question a lot, 'What's the difference between a shift in market structure
and just a run on stops?' Well, you have to have a macro understanding of where the market's likely to go,
and that's what I've spent the most time so far discussing today. What is the underlying narrative that I think
is going to be implemented, or what I'm going to submit myself to? And if it does this, then I'll engage this
week. If it doesn't do these types of things that I'm looking for, I will have nothing to share with you. I won't
be able to say, 'Here's a trade I took.' I won't be able to do any of that stuff. I won't be able to point to
anything on Twitter beforehand, like you see me do every single week when it happens. I'm going to be
reserved and quiet because I want to be watching and seeing what takes place geopolitically, on the
warfront, what's going on over in Gaza, what's going on with Iran, what's going on with all the other Arab
nations. I'm watching all of that. That's where my focus is. My students, I've been telling them, 'Watch the
Middle East because that's what's coming.' Well, now we're boiling over. Now that pot is boiling over, and
all of us are going to get burned by it.

- INEFFICIENCY AS POTENTIAL INVERSION FAIR VALUE GAP (IFVG)
Just because we live on the other side of the world doesn't mean anything. Think about how many people
have come through our Southern border. We don't know what's going on. We don't know what's going to
pop off here, but it could get real busy here real quick, and that could speed up a whole lot of fear in the
marketplace, and they will use and capitalize on that fear and reprice aggressively way beyond what you
expect, way, way, way beyond what you would expect in terms of your targets. So it's also occurring at a
time of the year when there's larger range expansions anyway. There's bigger movements in these months
going into the end of the year. So if we had a bullish dollar I'm sorry, if we had a bearish dollar and all
factors that I've mentioned, outlined here, were not so, and it was expected to go lower for the Dollar Index,
this would have been a bullish breaker, and it would have never went this low. Now, because there is this
small old gap right there, here's for your notes. That little inefficiency right there, right now, that's what it
keyed off of me. I have to put this border on it because if I don't do it, it's going to drive me nuts. So this
inefficiency, we traded down into it there, just a little bit past the midpoint or consequent encroachment of it.
When it's like this and I'm expecting the dollar to continue higher, it may need to consolidate a little bit, but
ultimately, I think it's going to go higher because there's no reason for anything to be expected to smooth
out. Like the stuff that's going over Israel and Gaza, that's not going to go away anytime soon. Like, there's
a lot of anger and a lot of pent-up hostility, and retributions are sought after. So that stuff's just beginning,
and because of that, I don't think that we're out of the woods yet in terms of the dollar going higher
because it will be treated as a safe haven.

- TRADING STRATEGY AND RISK MANAGEMENT
So if we are long-term bullish on dollar but just expecting some initial early week consolidation, early week
of this coming week is what I mean, then hopefully we start seeing the Dollar Index resume going higher
because that's the catalyst in the narrative that I think is in play or potentially in play this week. All of this
movement here above this high would never have me looking for this as a bullish breaker because we have
the low, high, lower low. We moved above it; it's a shift in market structure. No, there's no fair value gap in
here because we've had all this overlapped candlestick run running all the way over top this range here.
This candle here overlapped most of the upper portion of this wick here, and this candle we went down a
little bit and it went higher. So there's a whole lot of back and forth movement across this candle, this
candle, this candle, this candle, and this candle. Now, while we're dropping, to me, I think this is a
meaningful drop because I think we're going to go lower and take that low out. But since there's only this
gap here, we went over top of it here. We overlapped right there, went through it. Does that mean it's no
longer considered worthy? No, it means that my eye jumps right to this, and this becomes a potential
inversion fair value gap, meaning that I'm anticipating, I'm expecting it to do a move lower, come back up,
and treat it as resistance. I'd like to see if it retraces back up into it. I would like to see it stop at the halfway
point after moving below it. If it does that, then I'm really, really bullish on dollar. Then I'll be really, really
looking for shorts in NASDAQ.

- PRICE LEVELS AND IMPLICATIONS
You see how everything is blended together with this the whole explanation about what I'm looking for,
why I'm thinking it, why I expect it, why I'm cautious, what am I specifically waiting for? That's experience.
That's knowing what to do with the models. That's also knowing and trusting myself as an individual and
also respecting the increased measure of risk right now. I'm okay with letting a price run happen on a
Sunday. If we get an extreme gap opening somewhere and it starts running a lot, who cares? I don't care. I
don't need that to be profitable. I don't need that to define me as a good trader. You don't either. You want to
be able to trade in high probability conditions where the risk is realistic and manageable, not infinite. You
don't know where it's going to go. Like your risk right now, if you carried over a long or short from Friday,
you have no idea where we're opening up. That's gap risk. I don't want gap risk right now. When I trade, I
don't trade with micro lots. I'm trading where if I'm wrong and we gap a lot against me, that could be a lot of
money, and I don't want to have a lot of money taken from me in a condition where I have no control. I have
no control of where we're opening up on a new week. So I teach my students to know that there's no
mechanism, there's nothing I have as a tool or a repertoire no algo, no macro, no secret little thing I'm
never going to make public. I have zero tool for the opening price of the new week. I don't have that. That's
always going to remain a mystery. I'm not going to be able to figure that out. I tried, and I don't know how to
do it. So I've no longer invested any time and energy into it. I've wasted a lot of money trying to trade with
real things to build what I thought was a model, and they didn't hold up. So I'm convinced that that's not a
worthwhile venture to try to dig into figuring out. It's not necessary. Once it opens, then now we have
information. We can worry about what it does after that opening price is made and where we closed the
previous week. So that's your opening range new week opening gap.

So, a long way around the barn to say that this, because this gap is here, we've already went through it
here, we went down into it here. So if we go through it once more, I want to see it respect the midpoint of it
and not trade up to the upper high. It can do that, but why would I not want to see it trade the upper portion
of it? Because it would imply exceedingly weakness. Meaning that if we trade down on Sunday or Monday
rather, and we get back up into and touch the low of this inefficiency, which is this daily high up to the
midpoint, which I'll actually give you the level, I'm so nice. The level here at 1.04969, so let's just call it
104.97. As long as it gets to or remains below 104.97, if it starts to drop there, I will trust that it will remain
weak because I'll treat the upper portion of it as the same way I would do with a breakaway gap. It would
imply exceedingly weakness, and I would be bullish on dollar, and therefore I would look at every rally in
NASDAQ as a suspect rally to fade that, and then every time it creates a new fair value gap that would be a
short, I would be confident doing that. So I've already, I've literally outlined everything that I would like for
this week, what would constitute a high probability scenario, what am I waiting specifically for. It's not
random, it's very specific things, and unless it does these types of things, I would not take any action on it,
and regardless of whatever the market does, I can be comfortable and not have any FOMO. I'm not fearing
of missing out on any of these moves that take place because what I framed out is what I'm comfortable
with, and incurring risk around it is an acceptable thing for me.

section_title: TRADING WITH LOGIC AND ACCEPTING LOSSES
content:
These things may not be what you're subscribing to. It may not even be your model. You may have
something entirely different that you're trading with, and you think that what I just outlined here makes no
sense to you, and you wouldn't use it. That's your business; you're welcome to have that. But for me, for me
to have something where I'm going to show you an example, 'This is what I did, this is how I use the
information I talked about on Sunday before the market opened up,' because you can see right here, we're
doing this live. It's New York local time, it's 1:34 PM. The market has not opened up yet. I'm co-signing all
this before it happens. I don't know if I'm going to be correct. But notice this: I am disarming everyone.
There's nothing to hold on to to be right or wrong right now because I have laid down the foundations about
what it is I want to do to trade anything this week. If it doesn't do it all this week, guess what happens? I've
taken no trades. Are you willing to go through an entire week without taking a trade? Chances are, you're
probably not because you're all hopped up on goofballs, like you're gambling. Like you're trying to do
something because you have time to do it. I want to trade because I want to make money. I want to sleep at
night knowing that what I'm about to do and take a trade on, it's reasonable, that risk is reasonable.

- A HIGHER EDUCATION IN TRADING
If I'm risking $6,000 on a position that might seems, that might seem crazy to you, but that $6,000, if I do a
double portion of that same trade, it might be a $12,000 risk. My stop loss could be $12,000 in the size of
my position, but I'm comfortable taking on that risk. You can't even trade that in months of profit. But that's a
trade that I'm willing to accept if given these types of parameters, if they are met this way. I can take a trade
like that and feel confident that I can get 50,000, 75,000 out of the market off of that move. So everything's
relative, everything is a matter of what fits you. But everything I just said could be scaled down to you this
week. You could use everything I just outlined in the scope of a microlot, the smallest of the profit and loss
potential that's given to you by the risk and leverage that you use, whether it be Forex or Futures. You have
to scale it for what you're comfortable with. For me, because I know there's a likelihood of a lot of
excitement, a lot of big moves can happen, I'm not just going to sit down and wait for something that just
feels right. I need to know that I'm using the higher timeframes. I know that I'm referring to and conferring
with other markets that are indicating something else excited, and there's a lot of fever around fear right
now. Gold and dollar moved together, so I want to see which one continues from Sunday's opening into
Monday's trading and how we close on Monday. I'll have more insights about that, and then I guess
Monday, I'll do a review again on YouTube, and then we'll revisit all these things I've mentioned here and
how it relates to or if it's been negated by something else that takes place because, again, anything can,
anything can pop off and change everything I've outlined. But you have to have something in mind, what
you permit yourself to assume as risk, because if you don't do that, you're just going to chase price. You're
going to jump around from one model to the next or one approach to the next, chase somebody else's
opinion, whether it be my opinion or fade something else. You're not going to know what you're doing.
You're just going to be doing something for the sake of doing it versus investing with a logic behind what it
is you're doing but also comfortable accepting the risk. So if I'm wrong, I'm accepting the fact that I'm
wrong. If I if I take the trade based on the things I'm sharing, I'm okay being wrong. In that case, it doesn't
change my opinion about what I'm using to trade with. It doesn't hurt my feelings. I don't get mad and go
out there and go raging. I'm just simply focusing on what I believe would be the best of the best for me
going forward this week, and I just outlined that for you.

- SELL SIDE LIQUIDITY AND BREAKER ANALYSIS
Let's just drop down to an hourly chart, might as well just do a full run. All right, so I like the idea of the sell
side here. You see how nice that is? It's real obvious sitting there like that. So at some point, I want to see
us work below that this week. Depending upon where we open up on Sunday, there's a little bit of a range
there, it's not a lot actually, it's not much at all really. That's Forex, I'm thinking, uh, I'm looking at a Futures,
but that might be our initial draw, and then down here, I like that as well. So those are two levels I'd be
looking for. I like how we went up, traded into this breaker here. We have high, low, higher high, the down
- closed candle traded up into that with a CY. This candle here is a CY. Then we sold off, small little
inefficiency. We traded up into that here, and I'd like to see it remain heavy with a dollar that doesn't want to
go lower, and that would allow for Euro to trade down into the sell side liquidity. If we do rally, there's a little
bit of cleanness in here, you see how these are just a little too smooth for my liking. If dollar runs lower, say
it sharply runs lower into that daily inefficiency outlined for dollar, we could see the Euro Dollar come back
up and wipe out this area here because this would be trail buy stops on shorts, and getting above these
buy stops here goes right into this inefficiency right there. So for the dollar, I'm not going to go any lower
than the dollar, I'm I'm not going to go any lower than the hourly chart. I apologize. This area here are two
premium arrays that are, if we see that drop in dollar, we'll see Euro trade up into those levels there. So if
you're going to be a scalper and you're not using what I'm outlining here and waiting for just that, if we get a
drop in dollar and you can see it trading towards that daily inefficiency, this is where the draw is on Euro for
the retracement idea. Even if it does this here, it doesn't upset the idea of what I've outlined earlier because
ultimately, I think the dollar goes higher. So we could see it go up here, upset that, and then slowly drift
down, and then later in the week aggressively run for sell side here, here, and then, you know, what we get
after that.

- WEEKLY CHART OVERVIEW
All right, so let's close this one, go to Cable real quick. If you're finding this helpful, insightful, I appreciate if
you give it a thumbs up. That's just my little indication that you're receiving from me and not just wasting
time talking about something. If you're you're getting some value from it, that's the report card you give me.
That's our currency here; it doesn't cost you anything to do it. So we have an inefficiency here on the
weekly chart. We traded up into that last week actually. Go in a little bit more detail. Okay, so we have an
old low in here. We traded up into that inefficiency and old low, traded up into it, and then we went right
back down into this area here.

- CONSEQUENT ENCROACHMENT AND LOWER QUADRANT CLOSE
So if you look at the consequent encroachment of that wick, or I guess it would be called the tail, we didn't
quite get down into it, you see that. So I would want to see us go into this and have a close. Same thing,
just reverse it. Now, I want to see it close on a daily basis because we're looking at a weekly chart. Look up
in the upper left-hand corner, this is a weekly timeframe. I want to see a daily close below that 1.20, we'll
call it 78 level. If it closes below that, then I would feel reasonably confident that we're going to take the low
out here. And then look at these lows here, my eye jumps right to this and this, that and that. We have this
already traded to or almost right there, consequent encroachment. So it's this wick and this wick. So I look
at those two as relative equal, and because we have undelivered consequent encroachment on this wick or
tail, that is where I believe the draw would be on the downside. So we'll say this is it there with the
expectation that it would eventually pierce this wick's or consequent encroachment of this tail and then get
into this inefficiency down here.

- BREAKER AND INVERSION FAIR VALUE GAP (IFVG)
All right, so with that, let's drop down into the daily. We're almost halfway there folks, we're almost there. So
we had the buy side taken here, and as I mentioned with the Euro Dollar, much in the same vein, this
would have normally been a breaker where we had a low, high, lower low, and if it was bearish for dollar,
then this would have set up a bullish breaker. No necessity to trade down here at all because there really
isn't an ideal fair value gap. There is one here right in here, but that wouldn't, that would have never been
one for me to see as a shift in market structure and trade all the way back down into that level there. That's
a little too deep of a retracement. I'm not a harmonics trader. I'm not a deep 88 percent or 80 percent,
whatever it is, bat pattern or whatever it is, you know. I don't subscribe to zoo patterns. So this would be
treated by me, if it were to trade below it, the same way I outlined it for Euro. I would treat it as an inversion
fair value gap.

section_title: INTERMARKET RELATIONSHIPS FOR HIGH PROBABILITY TRADING
content:
Now, do you see what I just did there? That's intermarket relationships. So intermarket relationships or
intermarket analysis, using other markets to support an idea, not just simply looking at one pair or one
market for the simple sake of 'I see a pattern, I have time to trade, so let me just take a trade.' That's too
myopic. You have to blend a lot of things to get to what we considered by my definition high probability.
Now, it doesn't mean that what I just outlined here is going to pan out. It just means that that's what I'm
waiting for that would illustrate that this would be a high probability short for pound, and that means it would
confirm continued upside likely to see dollar going higher. Okay, so I've taken you through the whole
spectrum of using other markets to support or what would negate the idea of me eventually taking a trade
in the only market I'm interested in right now, which is NASDAQ. But I gave you the criteria in which that
you can study this going forward this week. See if it pans out, see if it offers anything to you, and then what
patterns that you are looking for as your model, do they manifest later on in the week using this
information? Okay, so it's an interactive study. It's something that is explained in detail here. There's finite
parameters. It's not a 'if it goes up or down I'm right either way.' I'm looking for higher dollar, but I'm also
teaching how I will and expect because many of you, if you look around, have asked, 'How do you know
when a fair value gap is not going to work?' I'm outlining it here.

- DYNAMIC AND INSIGHT
But when it fails, it doesn't mean it's discarded. This means that I'm going to treat it as an inversion fair
value gap a fair value gap that fails in its function of bullishness or bearishness. If it fails, it will become an
inversion fair value gap after it comes back to it. So there's no reason for you to be upset about taking a
trade and getting stopped out. If you try to use it as an entry to go long and it stops you out, you're going to
get mad and think everything doesn't work because of that. When it should be, 'Wow, okay, I got that
wrong. All I got to do is sit back and wait. When it comes back up and hits it, I'll treat it as a shorting
opportunity,' and you'll get it back and probably more. And you won't wrestle with this idea that 'I got to be
right,' because when we're wrong, it gives us insight. We just had to pay a premium for that insight; that's
all, that's all it is. And that's how you wrestle through fear of missing out, fear of losing. Losing sometimes is
going to cost money for you to get better perspective and a better insight. You're going to get a higher
education because you paid for it. It's just like college. You're willing to pay for all those extra credits beyond
grade school rudimentary classes in elementary school, and you go into debt to pay for that higher
knowledge, that higher education. When you're a trader and you take a loss, it's the same thing. That
transaction is you just paying for another class, and you paid for those credits. Now, at the end of that credit
or that that semester or course, you now have the insights that should have been gleaned from that
exposure to that study of whatever it is, whether it be a class or mathematics or science or whatever is
some prerequisite for your degree or major. You're paying for that insight. A loss in trading should be
treated the same way. It should never be permitted to be catastrophic where you're risking the entire
access to your funded account or it completely decimates your funded account combine or it blows your
account or you lose $40,000 because you didn't use a stop loss in your live stream and you delete the
stream and you pretend it never happened.
IFVG
- SELL SIDE INEFFICIENCY AND BALANCED PRICE RANGE
There's only buy side here on this candle passing through. We didn't get to it here, we went just shallow
into it here, but from this candle's low to that candle's high, there's only buy side. It was delivered only here
on buy side. So what is it missing? Sell side. So if it drops down, it offers that sell side to this candle's high,
so it reprices to that range low, which is this candle's high. So that fair value gap has been repriced to offer
the buy side that's been given here, and then sell side. So if it moves away from it, comes back up, what is
it going to act as? A balanced price range, which is the reason why I'm telling you that I would like to see it
only trade to halfway. It can trade up to the highest point, but if it tells me no, I'm not interested in going all
to the top of it after moving below it, if it hits the low and only stops at midpoint or just below it and then
starts to move lower, that's exactly what I want because it's signifying that the balanced price range is not
permitting price to come all the way back up to the high of it. It need not do that. Why? Because we've
already traded down to it a little bit in here, so we've already had sell side offered to that degree on that
candle's low. If, if, see this is the part you don't want to hear; you want me to get to the nitty-gritty and make
it easy here to buy, sell, and expect it. But if you want to learn how to make money and consistently know
what you're looking for, why am I using this candle? Why am I not doing this? And why am I expecting this
range to stay unfilled? And why should I expect this one to be filled? Well, how do, I'm teaching it to you,
but it can't be done in a five-minute video.

- PRECISION TRADING
If this candle didn't trade down into the upper portion of this fair value gap, if it didn't do that right there,
then the one pass through it where I expect the fair value gap to fail here, not offer support, then I would
treat the fair inversion fair value gap as it can go all the way to the high. Why? Because it wouldn't have had
this drop down in the upper portion of this range, see that. But because it did that, it's already offered a
measure of sell side. So between that candle's low, which I'll highlight here, what I'm saying is essentially is
I don't want to see it trade above that blue line. If it trades down, worst case scenario, it can create a
Mohawk, which is a little tiny little deviation above or below a level that would be the level I'm looking for to
be respected. But because markets are factoring a spread, and we all know Forex has a spread, I don't
care what they tell you, what they offer you, 'Oh, I have spreads that are this and whatever.' If the broker
wants to expand it on you, they're going to expand it on you, and it is what it is, unless you're using a limit
order. If you're using a market order, chances are that spread isn't going to be what you actually get, and
how often are you really checking to see what you paid in spread? You're not, you're just hoping to get in
there because your fear of missing out is so high. That difference between this candle's low and the high of
the fair value gap, which is this line here, so I'm going to highlight both levels. I don't want to see price, if it
trades down below it, I don't want to see it trade back up in between these two levels once we get below
this shaded area because the effects of this candle trading down to the upper portion of this fair value gap,
it's already offered on the basis of this move here, that candle right there, that day has already delivered
most of the upper portion of what would be this fair value gap. So because it has done that, I don't want to
see, if we trade below it and then comes back up and touches the low or halfway point, I want it to be
sensitive there and not trade any higher, but it can create a small little Mohawk just above that line there,
but not touch the upper portion. If it does that, that tells me it's exceedingly weak, and then therefore I
would expect dollar to accelerate higher, and then any shorts that I see in NASDAQ should be high
probability, and then this should sell off pretty aggressively reaching down for the sell side below over in
here.

section_title: FAIR VALUE GAP UTILIZATION AND EXPECTATION
content:
But if this candle hadn't done this very thing here, that means that the only thing that this fair value gap has
seen, again, this portion right here, imagine that's not there, say this candle had a low up up up here. That
means that this range only had buy side offered to this to this point here. So what's it missing? It's
inefficient in sell side because it's buy side imbalance. That means its direction was a closing candle higher.
The difference between the candle before it and the candle after it is that there's a little gap in between
where the market has to eventually reprice lower. We're not down there yet. So when you look at a fair
value gap, you have to see how was it treated, how was it utilized. It wasn't even touched here. I said that
wrong. It wasn't, it wasn't willing to trade down to the consequent encroachment here. It didn't come to the
very low of that candle here. It didn't respect that. It traded just inside the upper portion or upper half of that
gap that's created by this candle's high and that candle's low. So because it's done that, I don't want to see
it, once it trades back back back down below it, I don't want to see it come back up and touch the top. But if
this wasn't the case here, then I would expect, and I would try to take a short higher up above the
consequent encroachment of the fair value gap. And if I can get a Mohawk right here on the high of it, or if
it just pokes itself above it, many times you've seen me do. I I I'll add a partial and pyramid in that very thing
right there, and you've seen people ask, 'Like, why were you adding to it? It was outside the fair value gap.'
Because a Mohawk is expected, it's permissible. We allow for it. I'm not scared of it. I know it's likely to
occur. If I can get fortunate enough notice, I didn't say lucky if I was fortunate enough to get the fill on
that Mohawk that just punches just above that, that's superior entry. That's like, that is, that's the, that's part
of the game I play when I'm trying to get fills. Like, I I want to try to do that. Sometimes I'm not successful
all the time because the market's darting all around.

- BREAKER, FVG, AND SUNDAY TRADING CAUTION
And hopefully, I've made a major dent in your understanding about inversion fair value gaps here. I tuck it
into these long-winded videos because these are the details. These are the details that... let me make sure
I didn't break my phone here. I told myself, and I laid it on the armchair next to me, 'I'm probably going to
drop it,' and that's good. Just last until November 12th because I'm turning this off. So now you have
insights: when do I expect the fair value gap to fill? Why would I expect it to fail? Why would I like to see
things remain open? I covered all that right here. Okay, so in my book, when I talk about inversion fair value
gaps, I'm going to cite this very video where I walk you through the explanation with an example because
everything I just said in text, you might read it and say, 'I don't understand what he's saying,' but now you've
seen me outline it. You've seen me refer to it here in every example where I've ever shown a trade where
I've used it, and you watched me call an inversion fair value gap before the market itself respected it as
such. Now you understand the dynamic of what I trusted at the time because it's not make-believe, and it's
not hindsight. It's logic that's being referred to on an ongoing basis that will tend to repeat more times than
it will fail. And then, then and then of itself, that is an edge. That's a model. That's something you can you
can put statistics and probabilities behind. And if you can determine when the market's predisposed to go
higher or lower, that by definition gives you high probability. If you can't do your trades with that mindset
behind each and every one of them, you're gambling. You have no idea what you're doing. You're just
hoping and praying, and when it's right, you'll call it skill. When it's wrong, you'll blame me. You'll blame the
methodology, you'll blame the broker, you'll blame the market, you'll blame everything else but yourself. So
this discussion is just reverting back to you as the responsible party. When you do it wrong, you did it
wrong. Everything else is exactly what it is; it's separate from you. These gaps don't make you buy them.
They don't make you short them. You decide to. You make that decision. So I'll move on because I could go
on a lecture about that. So let's go into the 60-minute chart. All right, and same thing here. We had a CY
breaker. We traded up into both of those. That's a nice, that is a very, very strong model when you're
looking for sell side. Everything reversed, you can use it for when you're looking for buy side. It broke lower,
which hit up into the inefficiency here, and this is one of those things that, um, I got burned many times on
Sundays where I had this very scenario here where it looks like, okay, we traded up into that, and if we
opened up inside this fair value gap here, I would go short right there on Sunday and expect Sunday going
into Monday London, it would just trade lower. And it wouldn't do that. Sometimes it would go higher, take
the buy side that I would be treating as my stop, and totally prevent me from being profitable for that
Sunday's trading. It was this very thing here that caused me to stop trading on Sundays, where I used to
take trades on Sunday. When we would open up, I would trade the first three hours or so of Sunday and
then pack it up, going in around 10:00. You do whatever I normally do if I'm going to nap or what not before
London session, or if I went to sleep, that's what I would do, and then come back in London and see if...
